name: FFmpeg Video Production Guide
description: Comprehensive FFmpeg knowledge for video production — Ken Burns effects, transitions, text overlays, countdown animations, audio mixing, and YouTube Shorts composition

skill.name: ffmpeg_guide
skill.description: FFmpeg video production knowledge — filters, effects, transitions, composition techniques
skill.system_prompt:
## FFmpeg Video Production Reference

You have deep knowledge of FFmpeg for video production. Use `bash_execute` to run all commands. Always use `-y` to overwrite outputs.

### Output Format for YouTube Shorts
- Resolution: 1080x1920 (9:16 vertical)
- Framerate: 30fps
- Codec: libx264, preset fast, crf 18
- Audio: aac, 192k bitrate
- Max duration: 60 seconds

### Image Preparation
Scale any image to fit 1080x1920 (cover crop, no letterboxing):
`-vf "scale=1080:1920:force_original_aspect_ratio=increase,crop=1080:1920"`

For images that don't match 9:16, this scales up to cover the frame and crops the overflow. The subject stays centered.

### Ken Burns Effects (zoompan filter)
Creates cinematic motion from still images. Always calculate FRAMES = duration_seconds * 30.

**IMPORTANT — Preventing shaky/jittery zoom:**
The zoompan filter causes micro-jitter when x/y positions are fractional. To fix this:
1. Pre-scale the image to 4x output resolution BEFORE zoompan (gives sub-pixel precision)
2. Use `trunc()` on x and y to snap to integer pixels
3. Use small zoom increments (0.001 per frame, not 0.0015)

**Step 1: Pre-scale the image (do this ONCE before zoompan):**
`scale=4320:7680` (4x of 1080x1920) — this gives zoompan a much larger canvas to work with, eliminating jitter.

**Zoom In** — slowly pushes into center. Best for portraits, single subjects, dramatic reveals:
`scale=4320:7680:force_original_aspect_ratio=increase,crop=4320:7680,zoompan=z='min(zoom+0.001,1.12)':x='trunc(iw/2-(iw/zoom/2))':y='trunc(ih/2-(ih/zoom/2))':d=FRAMES:s=1080x1920:fps=30`

**Zoom Out** — starts tight, pulls back to reveal full frame. Best for establishing shots, finales:
`scale=4320:7680:force_original_aspect_ratio=increase,crop=4320:7680,zoompan=z='if(eq(on,1),1.12,max(zoom-0.001,1.0))':x='trunc(iw/2-(iw/zoom/2))':y='trunc(ih/2-(ih/zoom/2))':d=FRAMES:s=1080x1920:fps=30`

**Pan Left** — slowly moves from right to left. Best for wide landscapes, panoramas:
`scale=6480:7680:force_original_aspect_ratio=increase,crop=6480:7680,zoompan=z='1.0':x='trunc((iw-iw/zoom)*on/FRAMES)':y='0':d=FRAMES:s=1080x1920:fps=30`

**Pan Right** — slowly moves from left to right:
`scale=6480:7680:force_original_aspect_ratio=increase,crop=6480:7680,zoompan=z='1.0':x='trunc((iw-iw/zoom)*(1-on/FRAMES))':y='0':d=FRAMES:s=1080x1920:fps=30`

**Zoom to Top-Third** — zooms into upper portion. Good for faces, sky shots:
`scale=4320:7680:force_original_aspect_ratio=increase,crop=4320:7680,zoompan=z='min(zoom+0.001,1.12)':x='trunc(iw/2-(iw/zoom/2))':y='trunc(ih/3-(ih/zoom/2))':d=FRAMES:s=1080x1920:fps=30`

**Full command pattern** (image + audio → clip):
```
ffmpeg -y -loop 1 -i IMAGE.png -i AUDIO.mp3 \
  -vf "ZOOMPAN_FILTER" \
  -c:v libx264 -preset fast -crf 18 -c:a aac -b:a 192k -shortest -t DURATION OUTPUT.mp4
```
Note: The scale+crop is INCLUDED in the zoompan filter string above. Do NOT add a separate scale before it.
IMPORTANT: `-shortest` ensures video stops when audio ends. `-t DURATION` is a safety cap.

**Why this fixes shaking:**
- 4x pre-scale gives zoompan a 4320x7680 canvas — sub-pixel movements become full-pixel movements
- `trunc()` snaps x/y to integers — no fractional pixel wobble
- Smaller zoom increment (0.001 vs 0.0015) — smoother, less aggressive motion

**Fallback** if zoompan fails: drop it entirely, just use `scale=1080:1920:force_original_aspect_ratio=increase,crop=1080:1920` for a static frame. Still looks professional with subtitles.

### Text Overlays (drawtext filter)
Burn text onto video at specific positions and time ranges.

**Basic centered text:**
`drawtext=text='YOUR TEXT':fontfile=/System/Library/Fonts/Helvetica.ttc:fontsize=48:fontcolor=white:borderw=3:bordercolor=black@0.8:x=(w-text_w)/2:y=Y_POS`

**Timed text** (show only during a time range):
`drawtext=text='TEXT':...:enable='between(t,START_SEC,END_SEC)'`

**Position guide for 1080x1920:**
- Top title area: y=120, fontsize=62
- Upper content: y=200, fontsize=58
- Center screen: y=(h-text_h)/2, fontsize=54
- Lower content: y=h-300, fontsize=48
- Bottom subtitle bar: y=h-180, fontsize=44

**Text styling options:**
- `borderw=3:bordercolor=black@0.8` — black outline (readable on any background)
- `box=1:boxcolor=black@0.5:boxborderw=10` — semi-transparent box behind text
- `shadowcolor=black@0.6:shadowx=2:shadowy=2` — drop shadow

**Multiple text overlays** — chain with commas:
`-vf "drawtext=text='LINE1':...:enable='between(t,0,5)',drawtext=text='LINE2':...:enable='between(t,5,10)'"`

**Special characters** — escape colons and single quotes:
- Colon: use `\:` inside drawtext
- Single quote: use `'\''` or avoid them

### Subtitle Burn-in
Burn a short label at the bottom of each clip:
```
ffmpeg -y -i clip.mp4 \
  -vf "drawtext=text='LABEL':fontfile=/System/Library/Fonts/Helvetica.ttc:fontsize=48:fontcolor=white:borderw=3:bordercolor=black@0.8:x=(w-text_w)/2:y=h-180" \
  -c:v libx264 -preset fast -crf 18 -c:a copy output.mp4
```

### Countdown Number Animations
Create short (0.5-0.8s) number flash clips for list-format videos.

**Basic countdown clip:**
```
ffmpeg -y -f lavfi -i color=c='BG_COLOR':s=1080x1920:d=0.6 \
  -vf "drawtext=text='NUMBER':fontfile=/System/Library/Fonts/Helvetica.ttc:fontsize=300:fontcolor='NUM_COLOR':x=(w-text_w)/2:y=(h-text_h)/2,fade=t=in:st=0:d=0.15,fade=t=out:st=0.4:d=0.2" \
  -c:v libx264 -preset fast -crf 18 countdown_N.mp4
```

**With scale-up animation** (number grows in):
```
ffmpeg -y -f lavfi -i color=c='BG_COLOR':s=1080x1920:d=0.6 \
  -vf "drawtext=text='NUMBER':fontfile=/System/Library/Fonts/Helvetica.ttc:fontsize=300:fontcolor='NUM_COLOR':x=(w-text_w)/2:y=(h-text_h)/2,fade=t=in:st=0:d=0.1,fade=t=out:st=0.45:d=0.15" \
  -c:v libx264 -preset fast -crf 18 -pix_fmt yuv420p countdown_N.mp4
```

These need silent audio for concat. Add: `-f lavfi -i anullsrc=r=44100:cl=stereo -shortest`

**Full countdown clip with silent audio:**
```
ffmpeg -y -f lavfi -i color=c='BG_COLOR':s=1080x1920:d=0.6 -f lavfi -i anullsrc=r=44100:cl=stereo \
  -vf "drawtext=text='NUMBER':fontfile=/System/Library/Fonts/Helvetica.ttc:fontsize=300:fontcolor='NUM_COLOR':x=(w-text_w)/2:y=(h-text_h)/2,fade=t=in:st=0:d=0.15,fade=t=out:st=0.4:d=0.2" \
  -c:v libx264 -preset fast -crf 18 -c:a aac -b:a 192k -shortest countdown_N.mp4
```

### Concatenation
Join multiple clips into one video.

**Step 1: Create concat list file:**
```
file 'clip_0.mp4'
file 'countdown_5.mp4'
file 'clip_1.mp4'
...
```

**Step 2: Concatenate:**
```
ffmpeg -y -f concat -safe 0 -i concat_list.txt -c:v libx264 -preset fast -crf 18 -c:a aac -b:a 192k output.mp4
```

IMPORTANT: All clips MUST have the same resolution (1080x1920), framerate (30fps), and audio sample rate (44100). If concat fails, re-encode the problematic clip.

### Transitions

**Fade in/out** on the full video (most reliable):
```
-vf "fade=t=in:st=0:d=0.4,fade=t=out:st=TOTAL-0.4:d=0.4"
-af "afade=t=in:st=0:d=0.3,afade=t=out:st=TOTAL-0.3:d=0.3"
```

**Crossfade between two clips** (xfade filter):
```
ffmpeg -y -i clip1.mp4 -i clip2.mp4 \
  -filter_complex "[0:v][1:v]xfade=transition=fade:duration=0.3:offset=CLIP1_DUR-0.3[v];[0:a][1:a]acrossfade=d=0.3[a]" \
  -map "[v]" -map "[a]" output.mp4
```

**Available xfade transitions:** fade, fadeblack, fadewhite, dissolve, pixelize, wipeup, wipedown, wipeleft, wiperight, slideup, slidedown, slideleft, slideright, circleopen, circleclose, radial, smoothup, smoothdown

Note: xfade between many clips gets complex. For 5+ clips, simple concat + countdown flashes between scenes often looks better than complex transitions.

### Audio Mixing

**Mix background music under voiceover:**
```
ffmpeg -y -i video_with_voice.mp4 -i music.mp3 \
  -filter_complex "[1:a]volume=VOL,afade=t=in:st=0:d=FADE_IN,afade=t=out:st=TOTAL-FADE_OUT:d=FADE_OUT[bg];[0:a][bg]amix=inputs=2:duration=shortest[aout]" \
  -map 0:v -map "[aout]" -c:v copy -c:a aac -b:a 192k output.mp4
```

Volume dB to linear: -14dB=0.20, -16dB=0.16, -18dB=0.13, -20dB=0.10, -22dB=0.08

**Normalize audio loudness:**
`-af loudnorm=I=-16:TP=-1.5:LRA=11`

### Probing & Debugging

**Get duration:**
`ffprobe -v error -show_entries format=duration -of csv=p=0 file.mp4`

**Get resolution:**
`ffprobe -v error -select_streams v:0 -show_entries stream=width,height -of csv=p=0 file.mp4`

**Get audio sample rate:**
`ffprobe -v error -select_streams a:0 -show_entries stream=sample_rate -of csv=p=0 file.mp4`

**Check codec:**
`ffprobe -v error -select_streams v:0 -show_entries stream=codec_name -of csv=p=0 file.mp4`

### Common Pitfalls & Fixes

**Concat fails with "different codecs"** → Re-encode all clips with same settings before concat:
`ffmpeg -y -i problem.mp4 -c:v libx264 -preset fast -crf 18 -c:a aac -b:a 192k -ar 44100 fixed.mp4`

**Green frames / artifacts** → Add `-pix_fmt yuv420p` to the output

**Audio/video out of sync** → Add `-async 1` or re-encode with `-c:a aac`

**zoompan produces black frames** → Image might be too small. Pre-scale image larger first:
`ffmpeg -y -i small.png -vf "scale=2160:3840" big.png`

**drawtext special characters** → Escape: `\:` for colon, `\'` for quote, `\\` for backslash

**File not found in concat** → Use absolute paths or ensure relative paths are correct. Use `-safe 0` flag.

**Output too large** → Increase crf (e.g., crf 23) or use `-preset medium`

**No audio in output** → Check that `-map` includes audio stream. For lavfi color inputs, you must add a silent audio source.

### Performance Tips
- Use `-preset fast` for speed (good enough quality for Shorts)
- Use `-threads 0` to use all CPU cores
- Process clips in parallel when possible (they're independent)
- Use `-c:v copy -c:a copy` when you only need to change container, not re-encode
- Use `-c:a copy` when video filter doesn't touch audio (saves time)
